explain.rule: The first conditional is for real and likely situations. Structure: If + present simple, will + base verb. The if-clause NEVER uses will — it uses present simple even when talking about the future. The result clause uses will + base verb. Clauses can go in either order: 'If it rains, I'll stay home' = 'I'll stay home if it rains'. When the if-clause comes first, use a comma. When it comes second, no comma needed.
explain.why_matters: The first conditional is essential for talking about plans, consequences, warnings, offers, and promises. It is one of the most natural and frequent structures in everyday English.
explain.hebrew_note: Hebrew uses אם (im) + future tense for both parts: 'אם תלמד תעבור'. In English the if-clause uses PRESENT simple, not future. NEVER say 'If you will study, you will pass' — this is the most common Hebrew speaker mistake. The if-clause uses present simple: 'If you study, you will pass.' Think of it as: the condition is stated as a present fact, then you predict the future result.
explain.tip: Test: replace 'if' with 'when' mentally. If the sentence still makes sense, present simple is right. 'When it rains, I stay home' → correct. You would never say 'When it will rain' → so 'If it will rain' is also wrong. Also: unless = if not. 'Unless you hurry' = 'If you don't hurry'.

variations.example: If you're free, we could meet.
If you see him, tell him I called.
Unless you hurry = If you don't hurry

good_examples.sentence: If it rains tomorrow, we'll cancel the picnic.
good_examples.audio_text: If it rains tomorrow, we'll cancel the picnic.
good_examples.note: Present simple in if-clause. will in result.

good_examples.sentence: You'll pass the exam if you study hard.
good_examples.audio_text: You'll pass the exam if you study hard.

good_examples.sentence: If you don't hurry, you'll miss the bus.
good_examples.audio_text: If you don't hurry, you'll miss the bus.
good_examples.note: Negative in if-clause — still present simple

good_examples.sentence: If I get the job, I'll celebrate.
good_examples.audio_text: If I get the job, I'll celebrate.

good_examples.sentence: She'll be upset if she hears about this.
good_examples.audio_text: She'll be upset if she hears about this.

good_examples.sentence: If you see him, tell him to call me.
good_examples.audio_text: If you see him, tell him to call me.

good_examples.sentence: Unless you book in advance, you won't get a seat.
good_examples.audio_text: Unless you book in advance, you won't get a seat.

good_examples.sentence: If we leave now, we might make it.
good_examples.audio_text: If we leave now, we might make it.

good_examples.sentence: What will you do if you don't get the promotion?
good_examples.audio_text: What will you do if you don't get the promotion?

good_examples.sentence: I'll call you if anything changes.
good_examples.audio_text: I'll call you if anything changes.

bad_examples.wrong: If it will rain, I'll stay home.
bad_examples.correct: If it rains, I'll stay home.
bad_examples.reason: NEVER use will in the if-clause. Present simple only: if it rains.

bad_examples.wrong: If she will call, tell her I'm busy.
bad_examples.correct: If she calls, tell her I'm busy.
bad_examples.reason: If-clause always uses present simple, even when talking about future.

bad_examples.wrong: Unless you won't hurry, you'll be late.
bad_examples.correct: Unless you hurry, you'll be late.
bad_examples.reason: Unless already means 'if not'. Don't add another negative.

bad_examples.wrong: If you study, you would pass.
bad_examples.correct: If you study, you will pass.
bad_examples.reason: First conditional (real/likely) uses will. Would belongs to second conditional (hypothetical).

bad_examples.wrong: If I will finish early, I'll call you.
bad_examples.correct: If I finish early, I'll call you.
bad_examples.reason: No will in the if-clause. Ever. If I finish (present simple).

exercises.question: If it ___ (rain) tomorrow, the match ___ (cancel).
exercises.hint: If-clause = present simple. Result = will.
exercises.explanation: If + present simple + will + base verb.
exercises.hebrew_note: אחרי if = הווה פשוט. לעולם לא will

exercises.question: You ___ (pass) if you ___ (study) hard.
exercises.hint: Result = will. If-clause = present simple.
exercises.explanation: will pass (result) + if + study (present simple).

exercises.question: If she ___ (not / hurry), she ___ (miss) the train.
exercises.hint: Negative present simple in if-clause
exercises.explanation: doesn't hurry (negative present simple) + will miss (result).

exercises.question: ___ you ___ (call) me if you have any problems?
exercises.hint: Asking about a likely future action
exercises.explanation: Will you call? = asking for a promise/commitment.

exercises.question: If I ___ (get) the job, I ___ (take) you out to dinner.
exercises.hint: Real possibility about the future
exercises.explanation: get (present simple in if-clause) + will take (result).

exercises.question: ___ you ___ (see) him, ___ (tell) him to call me.
exercises.hint: Imperative (tell) in the result clause
exercises.explanation: If you see him, tell him — imperative as result.

exercises.question: ___ you ___ (book) in advance, you ___ (not / get) a seat.
exercises.hint: Unless = if not. Positive verb after unless.
exercises.explanation: Unless you book = if you don't book → won't get.

exercises.question: If the weather ___ (be) good, we ___ (go) to the beach.
exercises.hint: be in if-clause = is (present simple)
exercises.explanation: If it is good (present simple) + will go (result).

exercises.question: She ___ (be) upset if she ___ (hear) about this.
exercises.hint: Result comes first here — then the if-clause
exercises.explanation: will be (result) + if she hears (if-clause at end).

exercises.question: If you ___ (not / leave) now, you ___ (miss) your flight.
exercises.hint: Negative if-clause warning
exercises.explanation: don't leave (negative present simple) + will miss (result).

exercises.question: What ___ you ___ (do) if you ___ (lose) your job?
exercises.hint: Question about a real future possibility
exercises.explanation: What will you do (result question) + if you lose (if-clause).

exercises.question: If he ___ (not / apologise), she ___ (never / speak) to him again.
exercises.hint: Negative if-clause + strong result with never
exercises.explanation: doesn't apologise + will never speak.

exercises.question: If you ___ me, I'll be very grateful.
exercises.hint: If-clause → present simple, not will
exercises.explanation: present simple in if-clause. If you help (not will help).

exercises.question: Choose the correct first conditional:
exercises.answer: If she calls, I'll tell her.
exercises.hint: Present simple in if-clause + will in result
exercises.explanation: If she calls (present simple) + I'll tell (will).
distractors: If she will call, I'll tell her.
If she calls, I would tell her.
If she called, I'll tell her.

exercises.question: ___ you hurry, you'll miss the meeting.
exercises.hint: Unless = if not. Which word means 'if you don't hurry'?
exercises.explanation: Unless you hurry = If you don't hurry.

exercises.question: If it gets cold tonight, I ___ turn the heating on.
exercises.hint: might = possible but not certain. Less strong than will.
exercises.explanation: might = possible result. Less certain than will.

exercises.question: Which sentence shows a first conditional?
exercises.answer: If you eat too much, you'll feel sick.
exercises.hint: Real, likely situation → first conditional
exercises.explanation: Real possible situation + will = first conditional.
distractors: If you ate too much, you'd feel sick.
If you had eaten too much, you would have felt sick.
If you eat too much, you feel sick.

exercises.question: I'll send you the report if I ___ it in time.
exercises.hint: If-clause after the result clause — still present simple
exercises.explanation: Present simple in if-clause regardless of order.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'If it will rain, I'll stay home.'
exercises.answer: If it rains, I'll stay home.
exercises.hint: Will is forbidden in the if-clause
exercises.explanation: NEVER use will in the if-clause. Present simple only.
exercises.hebrew_note: אחרי if = הווה פשוט. לעולם לא will

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'If you study, you would pass.'
exercises.answer: If you study, you will pass.
exercises.hint: First conditional (real) uses will, not would
exercises.explanation: Real/likely situations → will. Would = second conditional (hypothetical).

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'Unless you won't be careful, you'll hurt yourself.'
exercises.answer: Unless you are careful, you'll hurt yourself.
exercises.hint: Unless already means if not — no extra negative
exercises.explanation: Unless + positive: unless you are careful = if you aren't careful.

exercises.question: Find and fix: 'If I will finish early, I'll call you.'
exercises.answer: If I finish early, I'll call you.
exercises.hint: No will in the if-clause — ever
exercises.explanation: If I finish (present simple) + I'll call (result).

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: If it rains, we'll cancel the picnic.
exercises.hint: If + present simple, will + base verb
exercises.explanation: If + present simple + will + base verb.

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: If you see him, tell him to call me.
exercises.hint: Imperative (tell) in the result clause
exercises.explanation: If you see + tell (imperative as result).

exercises.question: Put the words in the correct order:
exercises.answer: Unless you hurry, you'll be late.
exercises.hint: Unless + positive verb = if + negative
exercises.explanation: Unless you hurry = If you don't hurry.

speak.sentence: If it rains at the weekend, we'll stay at home and watch films.
chunks: If it rains at the weekend,
we'll stay at home and watch films.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Weekend' = WEEK-end. 'Films' = FILMZ — don't forget the plural Z sound.

speak.sentence: You'll feel much better if you get some sleep.
If she doesn't call me back, I'll send her a message.
chunks: If she doesn't call me back,

speak.sentence: Unless we leave now, we'll miss the last train.
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Unless' = un-LESS — stress on second syllable. 'Miss' = MISS.

speak.sentence: What will you do if you don't get into university?
chunks: if you don't get into university?
speak.phonetic_hints: 'University' = yoo-NIV-er-sih-tee — five syllables, stress on second.

speak.sentence: If I have enough time this evening, I'll call you.
chunks: If I have enough time this evening,
speak.phonetic_hints: 'Enough' = ih-NUF — stress on second syllable. 'Evening' = EEV-ning.

speak.sentence: The whole project will fail if we don't fix this problem quickly.
chunks: if we don't fix this problem quickly.

speak.sentence: If you see anything suspicious, please tell the security guard.
